coprinellus disseminatus.story: Unlike most inkcaps, this species does not deliquesce — its caps do not dissolve into inky liquid. It appears in enormous troops of hundreds or even thousands of tiny mushrooms on dead stumps and buried wood, making for spectacular photographic subjects.

coprinellus truncorum.story: This inkcap grows specifically on dead hardwood stumps and standing dead trees, often appearing in large clusters after rain. Like other Coprinellus species, it autodigests its gills to release spores, turning into black ink within hours of opening.

coprinopsis lagopus.story: The young fruitbody is covered in long, shaggy white hairs that resemble a rabbit's foot. It is one of the most ephemeral mushrooms — the entire lifecycle from emergence to complete deliquescence can occur within 24 hours, earning it a reputation as the ultimate 'now you see it, now you don't' fungus.

coprinopsis picacea.story: Named for its striking black-and-white patchwork pattern resembling a magpie's plumage, this is one of the most visually distinctive inkcaps. It is mildly toxic and can cause gastrointestinal upset.

cortinarius archeri.story: This vivid purple-violet Cortinarius is one of the most commonly photographed mushrooms in Australian eucalyptus forests. The genus Cortinarius is the largest genus of mushroom-forming fungi, with over 2,000 described species worldwide.

cortinarius semisanguineus.story: The 'surprise' is under the cap — while the cap is olive-brown, the gills are a vivid blood-red color. The red pigment is anthraquinone, the same class of compounds used in textile dyes, and this species has historically been used to dye wool and yarn.

cortinarius traganus.story: This lilac-colored webcap is unmistakable for its strong, unpleasant smell often compared to overripe pears or acetylene gas. Despite its beautiful color, the nauseating odor and bitter taste make it completely unpalatable, and it may be mildly toxic.

craterellus fallax.story: Nearly identical to the European C. cornucopioides, this North American species is among the most prized wild edibles but is notoriously hard to spot on the dark forest floor. Experienced foragers learn to 'see the shadows' — the mushrooms appear as vaguely trumpet-shaped dark patches among leaf litter.

crepidotus mollis.story: This small fan-shaped fungus looks like a miniature oyster mushroom but is distinguished by its brown spore print and gelatinous peel-able cap skin. It is one of the most common wood-decay fungi in temperate forests worldwide and is frequently misidentified as a young oyster mushroom.

cudonia circinans.story: This odd-looking fungus with a wrinkled, brain-like cap atop a slender stem fruits among conifer needle litter. It is suspected to contain gyromitrin-like toxins similar to those in false morels, making it poisonous despite its innocuous appearance.

cyathus olla.story: The 'eggs' in these tiny nest-shaped fungi are spore packets called peridioles, each attached by a tiny cord. When a raindrop hits the nest at the right angle, it launches a peridiole up to a meter away — a remarkable ballistic spore dispersal system perfected over millions of years of evolution.

cylindrobasidium evolens.story: This inconspicuous crust fungus on dead branches produces visible droplets of liquid on its surface through a process called guttation. It is one of the most common corticioid fungi in temperate forests but is usually overlooked by everyone except the most dedicated mycologists.

dacryopinax spathularia.story: This small yellow-orange spatula-shaped jelly fungus is pantropical and one of the most common jelly fungi in the world. In parts of East Asia it is harvested commercially as a less expensive alternative to Tremella fuciformis in soups and desserts.

desarmillaria caespitosa.story: Recently split from Armillaria, this species is the dominant honey mushroom in western North America. Unlike most honey mushrooms, it tends to fruit in dense clusters at the base of conifers and is considered a significant forest pathogen in Pacific Northwest old-growth forests.

disciotis venosa.story: This large brown cup fungus smells strongly of chlorine bleach, making it one of the most distinctively scented fungi. It fruits in spring near rivers and streams and is considered edible after thorough cooking, though the bleach smell persists until heated.

ductifera pululahuana.story: This translucent white jelly fungus grows on dead hardwood branches and is frequently confused with Tremella. It is pantropical and one of the most commonly photographed jelly fungi worldwide because its ghostly, brain-like appearance is striking on dark bark.

echinoderma asperum.story: Covered in pointed brown scales that give it a hedgehog-like texture, this woodland mushroom has a strong and unpleasant smell. It was formerly placed in Lepiota and is considered suspect — several closely related species contain amatoxins.

entoloma bloxamii.story: This magnificent steel-blue mushroom is one of the largest Entoloma species and is considered endangered in several European countries. It is an indicator of ancient, species-rich grasslands that have never been plowed or treated with artificial fertilizers.

entoloma hochstetteri.story: This entirely blue mushroom from New Zealand is so iconic that it appears on the country's banknote. The blue pigment is azulene-related compounds, a compound more commonly found in chamomile essential oil. Its gills are red from the pink spores, creating a striking blue-and-red combination.

exidia nigricans.story: This dark brown-black jelly fungus is one of the most common winter fungi on dead hardwood branches across the Northern Hemisphere. In European folklore, finding it on your door frame meant a witch had cursed your household, and the only remedy was to pierce it with pins.

exidia recisa.story: This amber-brown jelly fungus fruits on dead willow and other hardwood twigs in winter. It can completely desiccate to a thin dark film during dry spells and fully rehydrate with the next rain, resuming spore production as if nothing happened.

favolaschia calocera.story: Believed to be native to the tropics, this bright orange fungus has spread across the Southern Hemisphere and parts of Europe as an invasive species. It was first recorded in New Zealand in 1969 and has since been found in Australia, Italy, and East Africa, likely spread on imported timber.

flammulina populicola.story: Recently separated from F. velutipes by DNA analysis, this species grows specifically on cottonwoods and aspens in North America. It is one of the few mushrooms that can fruit during winter freezes, producing cold-tolerance mechanisms similar to the closely related F. velutipes that prevent ice crystal formation in its cells.

ganoderma sessile.story: This eastern North American reishi was long confused with G. lucidum until DNA work showed that true G. lucidum may be restricted to Europe. Most 'reishi' supplements sold in North America are likely derived from G. sessile or G. curtisii rather than true G. lucidum.

geastrum fimbriatum.story: When conditions are dry, this earthstar's rays curl inward, wrapping around the spore sac to protect it. When rain arrives, the rays unfurl and the exposed spore sac releases spores. This hygroscopic movement can repeat many times over weeks.

geastrum rufescens.story: This earthstar's pinkish-brown color when fresh distinguishes it from other species. Like all earthstars, it uses a bellows mechanism — raindrops hitting the papery spore sac force spores out through the apical pore in a visible puff that can travel over a meter.

geastrum saccatum.story: The most common earthstar in North America, frequently found under cedars and in sandy soils. Earthstars were used medicinally by several Native American nations — the some Native American nations used the spore powder as a wound dressing, as the fine spores helped stop bleeding.

gliophorus psittacinus.story: This mushroom starts vivid green and gradually shifts through yellow to orange as it ages, displaying the full color change of a parrot's plumage. The green pigment is unique among mushrooms and the chemistry behind it was only elucidated in the 2000s.

gloeoporus dichrous.story: This thin bracket fungus has a striking contrast between its white upper surface and pink-to-purple pore surface. It is one of the most common bracket fungi on dead hardwood in eastern North America and is often used as a teaching specimen for bracket identification.

gymnopilus luteofolius.story: This large, scaly, purple-tinged mushroom grows in dense clusters on dead hardwood and is one of the most commonly photographed Gymnopilus species. Some collections have been reported to contain psilocybin, though concentrations are inconsistent and sometimes absent entirely.

gymnopus androsaceus.story: This tiny mushroom grows on dead leaves and conifer needles, connected by wiry black stems as thin as horsehair. The stems are remarkably strong for their diameter and were traditionally used in Scandinavia as a thread for small crafts and fly-tying in fishing.

gyromitra caroliniana.story: This large brain-shaped false morel of the southeastern United States can weigh over a kilogram. Like other Gyromitra species, it contains gyromitrin, which metabolizes into monomethylhydrazine — a component of rocket fuel. Despite this, it is eaten in parts of the South after repeated boiling.

gyromitra infula.story: The saddle-shaped cap of this fall-fruiting false morel resembles a bishop's mitre. It contains gyromitrin toxin and has caused fatalities in Europe. Unlike spring-fruiting Gyromitra species, it appears in autumn on well-rotted conifer wood.

hebeloma radicosum.story: This large mushroom has a long rooting stem that extends deep into the ground, often into the abandoned burrows of rodents. It specifically colonizes decomposing latrines — the waste deposits of mice and voles — making it an indicator of underground animal activity.

helvella macropus.story: This grey saddle-shaped fungus on a fuzzy grey stem is one of the most commonly encountered Helvella species in temperate forests worldwide. The genus Helvella has been shown through DNA analysis to be far more species-rich than previously thought, with many cryptic species.

hemileccinum impolitum.story: This bolete smells distinctly of iodine or carbolic acid, especially at the stem base. Once placed in Boletus, DNA work moved it to its own genus. The chemical smell comes from phenolic compounds.

hemipholiota populnea.story: This scaly mushroom grows specifically on dead and dying poplars and aspens, and its presence often indicates that a poplar tree has internal decay. It was recently moved from Pholiota to its own genus based on molecular evidence.

hericium abietis.story: Unlike H. erinaceus which has long dangling spines, this western species has branched, coral-like architecture with short teeth. It grows exclusively on dead conifers in old-growth forests of the Pacific Northwest and is considered an indicator of forest antiquity.

heterobasidion irregulare.story: Recently split from H. annosum, this species is the most economically damaging forest pathogen in North America, causing billions of dollars in timber losses. It spreads through root contacts between adjacent trees and through airborne spores landing on fresh stumps.

hortiboletus rubellus.story: This small red-capped bolete is one of the most common boletes in gardens, parks, and along roadsides across Europe. Despite its cheerful appearance, it tastes bitter and like many fungi, may accumulate heavy metals from polluted urban soils.

hydnopolyporus palmatus.story: This unusual polypore produces tooth-like spore structures rather than pores or gills. Found in tropical and subtropical regions, it bridges the gap between polypores and tooth fungi, illustrating how spore-dispersal structures have evolved multiple times independently in fungi.

hygrocybe calyptriformis.story: Considered one of the most beautiful British fungi, this pink waxcap is a conservation priority species across Europe. It grows only in ancient grasslands that have not been plowed or fertilized for centuries, making it both a treasure and an indicator of irreplaceable habitat.

hygrocybe miniata.story: This tiny bright red waxcap is found on every continent except Antarctica, making it one of the most widespread waxcap species. Its vivid color fades to orange and then yellow as it ages, so a single cluster can display the full warm color spectrum.

hygrocybe pratensis.story: This buff-orange waxcap is the largest and most robust species in the genus, and one of the few waxcaps that is widely eaten. In Scandinavia it is gathered commercially and sold in markets. It is a grassland indicator species that signals ancient, unfertilized pasture.

hygrophoropsis rufa.story: Recently separated from H. aurantiaca by DNA analysis, this darker, more rufous species is actually more common than the classic false chanterelle in many regions. The split is a good example of how molecular tools continue to reveal hidden diversity in 'well-known' species.

hypholoma capnoides.story: Unlike its toxic lookalike H. fasciculare (which is bitter), this species has a mild taste and is a popular edible in central and eastern Europe. It grows exclusively on conifer wood and has greyish rather than green-tinged gills, which helps distinguish it.

hypoxylon fragiforme.story: These reddish-brown, globular fungi are among the most common organisms on dead beech wood in European forests. Despite their hard, charcoal-like texture when mature, they are active decomposers that break down lignin, the tough structural polymer that gives wood its rigidity.

inocybe erubescens.story: This is one of the most dangerous mushrooms in Europe, containing muscarine at concentrations that have caused fatalities. It fruits in parks under lime trees and has been mistaken for St. George's mushroom (Calocybe gambosa), which appears at the same time of year.

inonotus hispidus.story: This large, hairy bracket fungus grows high on living ash and apple trees, dripping brown liquid when actively growing. The extract has shown antimicrobial properties in laboratory studies, and the dried fungus was historically used as tinder and as a styptic to stop bleeding.

laccaria proxima.story: Larger and more fibrous-scaly than the common L. laccata, this species thrives in wet, boggy conifer forests. Like other Laccaria species, it is a powerful mycorrhizal partner and is used commercially in forestry for inoculating tree seedlings to improve survival rates.

lactarius chrysorrheus.story: When the flesh is broken, the white milk rapidly turns bright sulfur-yellow — a color change so fast and vivid that it looks like a chemical magic trick. This reaction is caused by enzymatic oxidation and is the quickest way to identify this common oak-associated species.

lactarius controversus.story: This large pinkish milkcap forms extensive fairy rings under poplars and willows and is one of the few Lactarius species with a peppery taste that is still eaten after salting. In Russia and Eastern Europe, salted milkcaps are a traditional preserved food served in winter.

lactarius glyciosmus.story: This small milkcap smells distinctly of desiccated coconut, making it one of the most pleasantly aromatic mushrooms in birch forests. The coconut scent is caused by lactones — the same class of chemical compounds responsible for coconut flavor in actual coconuts.

lactarius piperatus.story: The acrid, burning taste of this pure white milkcap is so intense it can numb the tongue. Despite this, it is traditionally eaten in parts of eastern Europe after prolonged soaking and pickling, which breaks down the irritant compounds.

lactarius pubescens.story: This birch-associated milkcap has a distinctly woolly, inrolled cap margin when young. In Finland, it is one of the species traditionally preserved by salt-curing, a technique that detoxifies the acrid milk and produces a fermented delicacy eaten throughout the long Nordic winter.

lactifluus volemus.story: Recently moved from Lactarius to Lactifluus, this species exudes copious quantities of white milk that smells of fish or crab when old. Despite the off-putting smell, it is one of the most highly prized edible mushrooms in East Asia, particularly in Yunnan province, China.

laetiporus huroniensis.story: This white-pored variant of chicken of the woods grows on hemlock in the Great Lakes region and was only described in 2001. Unlike the sulfur-yellow L. sulphureus, it causes gastrointestinal upset in many people, possibly due to compounds absorbed from the hemlock substrate.

leccinum holopus.story: This ghostly pale bolete associates exclusively with birch trees, particularly in boggy, wet habitats. Its nearly white cap and stem distinguish it from the more familiar brown-capped L. scabrum, though both are common birch-forest mushrooms across the Northern Hemisphere.

leccinum versipelle.story: This large, handsome bolete with its orange cap and scabrous black-scaled stem is one of the most popular edible mushrooms in Scandinavia and Russia. It must be thoroughly cooked — raw or undercooked specimens have caused gastrointestinal poisoning in multiple documented cases.

lentinellus ursinus.story: This fan-shaped, toothed-gill fungus grows on dead hardwood and tastes intensely bitter and peppery. Its serrated gill edges are its most distinctive feature and make it easy to identify under a hand lens.

leotia lubrica.story: These rubbery, yellow-green blobs on stalks look exactly like gummy candy growing from the forest floor. Despite the appealing name and appearance, they should not be eaten — they has uncertain edibility and is best avoided.

lepiota magnispora.story: This small dapperling with a yellow-brown cap and relatively large spores is common in European woodlands. The small Lepiota species are among the most dangerous mushroom groups in Europe — several contain amatoxins and have caused fatalities.

lepista personata.story: This robust grassland mushroom with its violet stem was so popular in the English Midlands that it was sold in markets in Nottingham and Leicester well into the 20th century. It fruits very late in autumn and can survive light frosts.

leratiomyces ceres.story: Originally from the Southern Hemisphere, this bright red-orange mushroom has become a common sight on wood chip mulch in gardens across Europe and North America. Its rapid global spread via the horticultural trade makes it one of the most successful invasive mushroom species.

leucoagaricus americanus.story: This large mushroom appears in gardens, compost heaps, and potted plants, and its flesh stains reddish-brown when bruised. It is one of the most common large mushrooms found growing in greenhouse soil and mulched garden beds in eastern North America.

leucocoprinus cretaceus.story: This fragile, pure white mushroom appears in flowerpots and greenhouses worldwide, having spread globally through the potting soil trade. It is one of several tropical Leucocoprinus species that have become virtually cosmopolitan through horticultural commerce.

lycoperdon excipuliforme.story: This tall, pestle-shaped puffball can reach 20 centimeters in height, making it one of the tallest puffball species. When its spore mass matures, the head crumbles away leaving a tall, persistent stem that can stand through winter like a small brown club.

lyophyllum decastes.story: Growing in dense clusters on disturbed ground, this mushroom earned its common name from its taste and texture when sautéed. It is a popular edible in Japan where it is known as hatakeshimeji, and it often appears along road shoulders, paths, and recently landscaped areas.

macrotyphula fistulosa.story: These slender, hollow clubs up to 30 centimeters tall grow from dead twigs and branches on the forest floor. They look like tiny organ pipes and are easily overlooked, but once spotted, they are found to be abundant in most temperate deciduous forests.

marasmius capillaris.story: One of the tiniest mushrooms commonly encountered, with caps barely 5 millimeters across, growing on individual dead oak leaves. The entire mushroom can dry out completely and revive with moisture — a trait called marcescence that lets it persist for weeks between rains.

meottomyces dissimulans.story: This obscure little brown mushroom was only recognized as a distinct genus in 2008. It grows on dead conifer wood and is one of many thousands of small brown mushrooms (LBMs) that challenge even expert mycologists to identify.

morchella americana.story: DNA analysis revealed that a common yellow morel of North America is not the European M. esculenta but its own distinct species. Morel hunters guard their spots fiercely — productive patches are passed down through families and some have been harvested annually for generations.

morchella punctipes.story: The cap of this morel is attached only at its very top, hanging like a skirt around the stem. It often fruits a week or two before the larger yellow morels appear, making it a useful harbinger that signals the main morel season is about to begin.

morchella tomentosa.story: This western species fruits prolifically in burned conifer forests the spring following a wildfire. Some foragers follow fire maps to predict morel hotspots the following season. Single burns in the Pacific Northwest have yielded commercial harvests of thousands of kilograms.

mutinus caninus.story: Smaller and more slender than the common stinkhorn, this species has a smaller, orange-tipped head covered in dark green spore slime. The fetid smell attracts flies which eat the slime and disperse the spores — an animal-dispersal strategy rare among fungi but convergent with certain tropical flowers.

mutinus elegans.story: This pinkish-red stinkhorn is one of the most common species found in eastern North American gardens and mulched beds. Like all stinkhorns, it emerges from an egg-like structure with remarkable speed — the fully formed mushroom can appear within just a few hours.

mycena epipterygia.story: The sticky, peelable skin on this tiny mushroom's cap is a distinctive feature. With over 500 described species, Mycena is one of the most species-rich genera of mushrooms, and most of them are small, delicate, and extremely difficult to identify without microscopy.

mycena galericulata.story: One of the most frequently encountered mushrooms on dead hardwood stumps throughout the Northern Hemisphere. It grows in dense tufts and varies enormously in color and size, which has made it a taxonomic headache — what we call M. galericulata may actually be a complex of several species.

mycena inclinata.story: Growing in dense tufts on dead oak stumps, this species is identified by its toothed cap margin and strong rancid or mealy smell. When fresh, the stem base often shows a distinctive orange-yellow tone that fades quickly after collection.

mycena interrupta.story: This tiny, brilliant blue mushroom from Australia and New Zealand is so photogenic that it has become an icon of Southern Hemisphere mycology. The blue pigment is unique to this species group and its chemistry is still not fully understood.

mycena lux-coeli.story: This Japanese bioluminescent Mycena glows bright green in the dark and is found on dead Chinquapin wood. The enzyme responsible for the glow, a fungal luciferase enzyme, has potential biotechnology applications and researchers have used fungal bioluminescence genes to create glowing plants.

mycena rosea.story: This beautiful pink Mycena is common in European beech forests and contains small amounts of muscarine. It is frequently confused with the also-pink M. pura, and both should be avoided as neither is safe to eat.

mycena sanguinolenta.story: When the stem of this tiny mushroom is broken, it exudes a dark reddish-brown juice that looks like blood. It is one of the most common Mycena species in conifer forests and its 'bleeding' habit makes it unmistakable among the hundreds of look-alike bonnet species.

mycetinis alliaceus.story: This small mushroom smells so strongly of garlic that it can be detected from meters away in a forest. It has been used as a garlic substitute in cooking in parts of Europe, as the flavor survives drying and even intensifies when the mushroom is powdered.

neonothopanus gardneri.story: This Brazilian bioluminescent mushroom glows bright green at night to attract insects that disperse its spores — one of the few fungi proven to use light for this purpose. Researchers demonstrated this by placing glowing and non-glowing artificial mushrooms in the forest and counting insect visits.

neofavolus alveolaris.story: The large, angular pores on this small bracket fungus look like honeycomb cells, giving it one of the most distinctive pore surfaces of any polypore. It grows on dead hardwood sticks and small branches and is common throughout eastern North America in spring.

ophiocordyceps unilateralis.story: This parasitic fungus infects carpenter ants and manipulates their behavior — the infected ant climbs to a precise height, clamps its jaws on a leaf vein, and dies as the fungus erupts from its head to release spores. This 'zombie' manipulation has been occurring for at least 48 million years based on fossilized bite marks on leaves.

orbilia xanthostigma.story: This tiny orange disc fungus has a hidden talent — its microscopic mycelium produces adhesive traps that capture and digest soil nematodes. It is one of over 200 known nematode-trapping fungi, which are being researched as biological control agents for parasitic roundworms.

oxyporus populinus.story: This bracket fungus grows primarily on living maple trees and is almost always covered in a thick mat of moss, making it look like a mossy shelf rather than a fungus. It indicates heartwood decay but infected trees can persist for decades before structural failure.

panaeolus cinctulus.story: This common lawn mushroom is one of the most widespread psilocybin-containing species in the world, found on every inhabited continent. It grows in fertilized lawns, parks, and gardens and is often unknowingly mowed over by millions of homeowners every year.

panaeolus sphinctrinus.story: This small brown mushroom grows on dung and heavily manured soil worldwide. Its mottled gills — where patches of spore-producing tissue mature at different rates creating a mosaic pattern — are the defining feature of the entire Panaeolus genus.

paralepistopsis amoenolens.story: This rare Mediterranean species contains acromelic acid, a neurotoxin that causes delayed-onset erythromelalgia — an agonizing burning pain and reddening of the extremities that can last weeks to months. Similar poisoning from the related Clitocybe acromelalga has been documented in Japan.

parasola conopilea.story: This delicate mushroom with deeply grooved cap and hair-thin stem appears overnight on dead wood and woodland soil. Like other Parasola species, it is so fragile that it collapses within hours of maturity, making it a 'ghost' that most people walk past without ever noticing.

parasola plicatilis.story: This tiny, deeply grooved mushroom appears on lawns overnight after rain and collapses by midday. Unlike true inkcaps, it does not deliquesce into ink. Its cap starts closed like a tiny parasol and opens fully flat before wilting, completing its entire visible life cycle in about 12 hours.

paxillus rubicundulus.story: This species grows exclusively with alders and was formerly lumped with P. involutus. Like P. involutus, it contains antigens that can cause a cumulative immune reaction — repeated consumption leads to increasingly severe hemolytic anemia that can be fatal.

peniophora quercina.story: This purple-grey crust fungus on dead oak branches is one of the most common corticioid fungi in temperate forests. Despite being everywhere, corticioid fungi are perhaps the most overlooked group of mushrooms — a single fallen log may host dozens of species that most people never notice.

phallus duplicatus.story: This stinkhorn has a delicate white net or 'indusium' hanging from beneath its cap, creating one of the most elegant structures in the fungal kingdom. The net may help waft the fetid spore-bearing slime to attract more flies from a greater distance.

phallus hadriani.story: This stinkhorn produces a pink-purple egg rather than the white egg of P. impudicus, and it tends to grow in sandy soils, dunes, and mulched gardens. The egg stage is eaten as a delicacy in parts of China and Europe, where it is sliced and served in salads.

pholiota aurivella.story: This large, slimy, golden-scaled Pholiota grows high up on living hardwood trees, particularly beech and birch. In Japan, a closely related species is cultivated and sold as 'nameko,' one of the country's most popular culinary mushrooms.

physarum polycephalum.story: This yellow slime mold is the species used in the famous maze-solving experiments at Hokkaido University. A single-celled organism with no brain, it can find the shortest path through a maze to reach food and has been shown to optimize network designs comparable to human-engineered infrastructure.

pleurotus dryinus.story: Unlike most oyster mushrooms, this species has a partial veil when young that leaves a ring on the stem. It grows on living and dead hardwoods and is edible but tougher than the common oyster mushroom, best when young and tender.

pluteus leoninus.story: This bright yellow Pluteus is one of the most vivid mushrooms on dead hardwood in European forests. The genus Pluteus is entirely saprotrophic on wood, making it unusual among gilled mushrooms — most of its look-alikes are mycorrhizal or soil-dwelling.

polyporus arcularius.story: This small, fringed-cap polypore on dead hardwood twigs has characteristically large, angular pores and a fringed cap margin. It is one of the earliest polypores to appear in spring and is found across North America, Europe, and Asia.

polyporus badius.story: The distinctive black-colored stem base of this otherwise brown polypore makes it instantly recognizable. It is a spring-fruiting species on dead hardwood and produces some of the most perfectly formed, symmetrical fruitbodies in the polypore world.

psathyrella piluliformis.story: This extremely common mushroom grows in dense clusters on dead hardwood stumps and buried wood. Its hygrophanous cap — which changes color as it dries — and fragile, crumbling flesh make it easy to recognize at the genus level, but Psathyrella species are notoriously difficult to identify to species.

pseudoclitocybe cyathiformis.story: This dark brown, goblet-shaped mushroom is one of the few species that can be found year-round in temperate forests, even fruiting during mild winter spells. Its persistent fruitbodies can survive multiple freeze-thaw cycles.

psilocybe aztecorum.story: This high-altitude species grows above 3,000 meters on volcanic slopes in central Mexico and may have been one of the teonanacatl mushrooms used in Aztec ceremonial practices. Aztec codices and Spanish colonial accounts describe ritual mushroom consumption during coronation and religious ceremonies.

psilocybe cyanescens.story: This potent psilocybin mushroom has become a common urban species, thriving on wood chip mulch in gardens and parks across the Pacific Northwest and western Europe. Its distinctive wavy cap margin and strong blue-bruising reaction on the stem are key identification features.

ramaria flava.story: This large, bright yellow coral fungus is one of the most commonly encountered Ramaria species in temperate forests. Some Ramaria species are good edibles while others cause gastrointestinal upset, and the genus is so taxonomically confused that confident identification is extremely difficult.

ramaria formosa.story: Despite its appealing name and salmon-pink color, this coral fungus is a well-known gastrointestinal toxin that causes severe nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. It has tricked many foragers who assumed all coral fungi were safe to eat.

rhizomarasmius pyrrhocephalus.story: This tiny mushroom with a reddish-brown cap and wiry black stem grows on dead leaves and fine woody debris. Like many small marasmioid fungi, it plays a disproportionately important role in leaf litter decomposition in temperate and tropical forests.

rickenella fibula.story: This tiny orange mushroom grows exclusively among mosses, where it associates intimately with mosses, possibly feeding on their rhizoids. It is one of the most common and widespread moss-associated fungi in the Northern Hemisphere but is often overlooked due to its miniature size.

russula aeruginea.story: This green-capped Russula is one of the most common species in birch forests across the Northern Hemisphere. It is a good edible with a mild taste, but green-capped Russulas must be carefully distinguished from the deadly Amanita phalloides, which can look superficially similar.

russula atropurpurea.story: This dark-capped Russula is one of the most common species in European oak forests. The genus Russula contains over 750 described species worldwide, making it one of the most species-rich genera of ectomycorrhizal fungi, and identification often requires microscopy and chemical tests.

russula mairei.story: This red-capped Russula grows exclusively with beech trees and causes vomiting if eaten raw. The 'sickener' group of red Russulas all contain sesquiterpene compounds that irritate the gastrointestinal tract but are broken down by thorough cooking in some species. Some authorities now place this species under the name Russula nobilis.

russula ochroleuca.story: Possibly the most common Russula in European forests, this yellow-capped species is found with both conifers and hardwoods. It has a slightly acrid taste and is generally avoided by foragers, though it is not considered dangerous.

russula rosea.story: This firm, pink-capped Russula with its distinctly hard, compact flesh is one of the most robust species in the genus. It associates with beech and oak in Europe and is considered a good edible, though it is not widely collected.

russula sardonia.story: The intensely acrid taste of this purple-capped pine forest Russula can make your entire mouth burn for minutes. Its tear-inducing pungency is a useful identification feature — it is one of the most bitter-tasting mushrooms in the genus.

russula vesca.story: Named 'vesca' meaning 'edible' in Latin, this is widely considered the best-tasting Russula — nutty and mild with a firm, crunchy texture. A distinctive feature is that the cap skin falls short of the margin, exposing the bare white flesh beneath like a ring of tiny teeth.

sarcoscypha austriaca.story: Nearly identical to S. coccinea, this scarlet cup fungus can only be reliably distinguished by microscopic spore features. Both species are among the first fungi to fruit in late winter, their brilliant red cups appearing on mossy sticks while snow is still on the ground.

scleroderma areolatum.story: Named for its leopard-spot patterned surface, this earthball is common in parks and gardens. All Scleroderma species are mildly toxic and can cause gastrointestinal upset — they have been used fraudulently as a cheap substitute for truffles, a practice that is both illegal and dangerous.

scleroderma verrucosum.story: Distinguished from S. citrinum by its warty surface and distinct stem-like base, this earthball is common on sandy soils. Despite being toxic, it forms ectomycorrhizal relationships with trees and is actually beneficial to forest health through its root partnerships.

serpula lacrymans.story: The most destructive building fungus in the world, this species has has caused enormous structural damage across Europe. It can transport water through its rhizomorphs across meters of dry masonry to reach new wood, earning the terrifying ability to 'irrigate' its own food supply.

spongipellis pachyodon.story: This rare tooth fungus produces large, soft, white fruitbodies that look remarkably like marshmallows growing from hardwood trees. It is considered a species of conservation concern in several states and its presence indicates an old-growth or mature forest stand.

steccherinum ochraceum.story: This crust-like fungus with tiny teeth instead of pores or gills is one of the most common tooth fungi on dead hardwood branches. Its orange-ochre color and tooth-bearing surface make it easy to identify with a hand lens.

stereum complicatum.story: This thin, bracket-like crust fungus grows in overlapping masses on dead hardwood and is extremely common in eastern North American forests. It bleeds orange when fresh, a useful field character that distinguishes it from similar stereum species.

stereum ostrea.story: Frequently confused with the true turkey tail (Trametes versicolor), this species lacks the pore surface underneath — it has a smooth underside instead. The easiest way to distinguish them is to flip the bracket over: pores mean true turkey tail, smooth means false.

stropharia ambigua.story: This large yellow Stropharia with shaggy white veil remnants on its cap margin is common on wood chips and garden mulch in western North America. Its common and scientific names both reference the uncertainty about its edibility — best avoided.

suillellus luridus.story: This bolete's flesh turns intensely blue when cut — one of the most dramatic color changes in the mushroom world. The blue staining is caused by oxidation of variegatic acid to quinone methide. Despite its toxic-looking color change, it is eaten in parts of Europe after thorough cooking.

suillus collinitus.story: This slimy-capped bolete associates with pines across Europe and has pinkish-lilac mycelial strands at the stem base. The genus Suillus is remarkable for its strict host specificity — each species partners with only one or a few tree genera, following the trees wherever they have been planted worldwide.

suillus spraguei.story: Formerly known as S. pictus, this beautiful red-scaled bolete associates exclusively with eastern white pine. It is one of the most distinctively patterned boletes in North America, with its red fibrous cap scales over a yellow background.

trametes hirsuta.story: This grey, coarsely hairy bracket fungus is one of the most common polypores on dead hardwood worldwide. It is frequently confused with turkey tail but is thicker, greyer, and hairier, with larger pores visible to the naked eye.

trametes ochracea.story: Very similar to turkey tail but with a more uniformly ochre-brown color and less vivid zoning. Recent DNA studies suggest that what we call T. ochracea may actually be a color form of T. versicolor rather than a separate species.

trametes pubescens.story: This thin, white to cream bracket fungus is distinguished by its velvety upper surface and relatively large pores. It is common on dead birch and other hardwoods and has shown promise in laboratory studies for producing enzymes useful in bioremediation of industrial pollutants.

tremella encephala.story: This pale pink, brain-shaped jelly fungus is actually a parasite of other fungi — specifically it grows on Stereum sanguinolentum on conifer wood. The relationship between Tremella species and their host fungi is an example of mycoparasitism that is far more common in nature than most people realize.

trichaptum biforme.story: The purple-toothed pore surface of fresh specimens makes this one of the most beautiful common bracket fungi. As it ages, the purple fades to brown, but young specimens are frequently photographed for their vivid color. It is one of the first colonizers of dead hardwood.

tricholoma sejunctum.story: This greenish-yellow Tricholoma has fooled many foragers who confused it with edible species. Its slightly bitter taste and streaky cap help identify it. The genus Tricholoma is one of the most important ectomycorrhizal groups in temperate forests worldwide.

tricholoma vaccinum.story: This shaggy-capped Tricholoma associates specifically with spruce trees in mountain forests. Its woolly, reddish-brown scales give it a distinctive unkempt appearance. It is bitter-tasting and not considered edible.

tricholomopsis decora.story: Named for its purple-brown and yellow coloring reminiscent of the British dessert, this striking mushroom grows on dead conifer wood. Unlike the mycorrhizal Tricholoma, Tricholomopsis species are wood decomposers — a different ecological strategy despite the similar appearance.

tubifera ferruginosa.story: In its early stage, this slime mold looks exactly like a cluster of tiny bright pink-red berries, leading many people to post excited photos of mysterious 'berries' growing on logs. It is one of the most commonly photographed and misidentified slime molds.

tylopilus chromapes.story: The bright yellow-chrome base of this bolete's stem is its most distinctive feature and the source of its name. It has a distinctive pinkish spore print and slightly bitter taste, and is one of the most commonly encountered Tylopilus species in eastern North American oak-hickory forests.

tyromyces fissilis.story: This soft, white bracket fungus crumbles like cheese when touched and has a distinctive sour smell. It is common on dead hardwoods and is one of the fastest-growing polypores — a fruitbody can reach full size in just a few days.

vascellum pratense.story: This small puffball is the most common puffball species on mowed lawns and sports fields across North America and Europe. It has a distinctive diaphragm — an internal membrane separating the spore mass from the sterile base — that distinguishes it from true Lycoperdon puffballs.

volvariella bombycina.story: This large, silky white mushroom emerges from a conspicuous sac-like volva high up on dead or dying hardwood trees. It is one of the most spectacular mushrooms to find in urban settings, sometimes appearing on dead elms and maples in parks and along streets.

xerocomellus cisalpinus.story: Recently split from X. chrysenteron by DNA analysis, this species is distinguished by its blue-green staining at the stem base. It is common in urban parks and gardens across Europe and North America and is one of many cryptic bolete species revealed by molecular taxonomy.

xeromphalina campanella.story: These tiny orange bells growing in vast colonies on dead conifer logs are one of the most charming sights in northern forests. A single rotting log can host thousands of fruitbodies, creating a miniature landscape that looks like a field of tiny flowers.

xeromphalina kauffmanii.story: Larger and browner than its common relative X. campanella, this species grows on hardwood rather than conifer logs. It was named after the pioneering American mycologist Calvin Henry Kauffman, who described hundreds of mushroom species from Michigan in the early 20th century.

xerula megalospora.story: This mushroom has a long rooting stem that extends deep underground to reach buried dead wood. Recently split from X. radicata by molecular work, it is one of several cryptic species in the rooting shank complex that look identical in the field.

xylaria longipes.story: Thinner and more elongated than the related X. polymorpha (dead man's fingers), this species grows specifically on dead sycamore and beech wood. The two species are often confused, but X. longipes has a distinctly longer, more slender form and a preference for different wood.

amanita submembranacea.story: This common boreal species has a distinctive grey universal veil that leaves a patch on the cap and a sock-like volva at the stem base. It is widespread in spruce and birch forests across northern Europe and North America but is poorly known due to longstanding confusion with similar species.

boletus reticuloceps.story: This prized edible bolete from East Asia has a distinctive reticulate (net-like) pattern across its entire cap surface. It is commercially harvested in China and Japan and is considered comparable in quality to the European porcini.

buglossoporus quercinus.story: One of the rarest bracket fungi in Europe, this species grows only on ancient oaks and is a priority species for conservation in the UK. Its decline tracks the loss of old-growth oak woodlands across western Europe over the past centuries.

callistosporium luteo-olivaceum.story: This small olive-yellow mushroom on conifer wood is easily overlooked but has attracted attention for its distinctive almond-like smell. It was recently shown to be more closely related to Mycena than to its former taxonomic neighbors.

cantharellus californicus.story: This enormous chanterelle from California's live oak forests can weigh over a kilogram — making it the largest chanterelle species in the world. It was only formally described in 2008 despite being well known to West Coast foragers for decades.

cantharellus enelensis.story: One of many chanterelle species revealed by DNA analysis in the 2010s, demonstrating that what foragers called 'the chanterelle' was actually dozens of distinct species with different geographic ranges and host tree preferences.

chroogomphus vinicolor.story: This pine-associated species has a distinctive wine-red to vinaceous cap and is one of the few genera in the Gomphidiaceae that parasitizes the mycorrhizal relationship between Suillus fungi and pine trees — it steals nutrients from another mushroom's tree partnership.

ciboria batschiana.story: This small brown cup fungus grows exclusively from buried acorns and is responsible for the 'mummification' of acorns in leaf litter. It is one of many specialized fungi that have evolved to exploit a single specific substrate.

clathrus archeri.story: Originally from Australia, this alien-looking stinkhorn with its red tentacle-like arms has invaded Europe and the Americas. It emerges from a white egg and unfurls like something from a science fiction film, releasing a putrid smell to attract spore-dispersing flies.

clitocybe fragrans.story: This small, pale funnel cap smells strongly of anise and is common in late autumn in deciduous and coniferous forests. Despite its pleasant scent, it should be avoided — it is difficult to distinguish from toxic Clitocybe species that also grow in similar habitats.

collybia cirrhata.story: This tiny white mushroom grows from the blackened remains of other decomposed mushrooms, connected by fine white threads. It produces small, round sclerotia — hardened resting bodies — that can survive drought and cold, germinating when conditions improve.

cortinarius armillatus.story: The distinctive red-orange bands around this webcap's stem are remnants of the cortina (veil) and make it one of the easiest Cortinarius species to identify in birch forests. Despite being recognizable, it should not be eaten — many Cortinarius species are kidney-toxic.

cortinarius sanguineus.story: Every part of this mushroom is blood red — cap, gills, stem, and flesh. The pigment is a potent anthraquinone dye that has been used for centuries in Scandinavia to dye wool, producing colors ranging from crimson to rust depending on the mordant used.

craterellus ignicolor.story: This tiny, bright orange chanterelle relative grows in mossy oak forests of eastern North America. It is edible but so small that gathering enough for a meal requires considerable patience, and it is more often admired than collected.

cystoderma amianthinum.story: This small, yellow-brown mushroom covered in granular scales is common in mossy grasslands and heaths. Its granular cap and stem surface is caused by chains of inflated cells that break apart into a powder when touched.

dacrymyces stillatus.story: These tiny orange jelly dots on dead conifer wood are among the most overlooked yet abundant fungi in the world. They appear year-round and can produce millions of spores from a fruitbody smaller than a pea.

entoloma sericeum.story: This common grassland mushroom is one of the most frequently encountered Entoloma species but is mildly toxic, causing gastrointestinal upset. The pink spore print that gives the 'pinkgill' name to the genus is a key feature for identifying all Entoloma species.

galerina vittiformis.story: This tiny moss-dwelling Galerina is one of the most common species in the genus but unlike the deadly G. marginata, it grows on moss rather than wood and does not contain amatoxins. Proper identification is critical as confusion between Galerina species can have fatal consequences.

gomphus floccosus.story: Despite its chanterelle-like shape, this large, scaly, vase-shaped fungus causes gastrointestinal upset in many people. It is common in western North American conifer forests and its orange, scaly cap makes it one of the most distinctive fungi in its range.

gymnopilus sapineus.story: This bitter orange-brown mushroom is extremely common on dead conifer wood across the Northern Hemisphere. Unlike G. junonius, this species does not contain psilocybin, despite persistent misidentifications by hopeful foragers.

hebeloma sinapizans.story: One of the largest Hebeloma species, this mushroom has a bitter, radish-like taste and exudes clear droplets on its gills when young. The genus Hebeloma is notoriously difficult to identify and has been called 'the most taxonomically challenging genus in the Agaricales.'

hemistropharia albocrenulata.story: This unusual species was bounced between Pholiota and Stropharia before landing in its own genus. Its slimy cap and distinctive white-fringed gill edges make it recognizable on dead hardwood in eastern North American forests.

hydnum umbilicatum.story: This small, orange hedgehog mushroom with a depressed cap center is common in eastern North American hardwood forests. Like all hedgehog mushrooms, it has spines instead of gills underneath, making it one of the safest groups for beginner foragers since no poisonous species share this feature.

hygrocybe ceracea.story: This small, waxy yellow mushroom is common in unimproved grasslands across Europe. Waxcap grasslands — old pastures with diverse populations of Hygrocybe species — are now recognized as a habitat of European conservation importance, equivalent in fungal biodiversity to ancient woodland.

hypholoma sublateritium.story: This brick-red capped mushroom growing in dense clusters on dead hardwood stumps is one of the most common mushrooms in temperate forests worldwide. In Japan it is considered edible and is commercially cultivated, though in Europe it is generally avoided due to its bitter taste.

inocybe lilacina.story: This beautiful lilac-colored Inocybe is commonly mistaken for the edible wood blewit (Lepista nuda). The confusion is dangerous — most Inocybe species contain muscarine, and this genus accounts for a significant proportion of mushroom poisonings requiring hospitalization in Europe.

kuehneromyces vernalis.story: This spring-fruiting lookalike of K. mutabilis and G. marginata grows on dead hardwood in eastern North America. The near-identical appearance of edible and deadly species in this group makes it one of the most dangerous identification challenges for foragers.

laccaria tortilis.story: The smallest Laccaria species, this tiny mushroom with a wavy, irregular cap grows on bare, compacted soil. Despite its minuscule size, it is an effective mycorrhizal partner for trees and is used in forestry nurseries for seedling inoculation.

lactarius blennius.story: This slimy, olive-grey milkcap grows exclusively under beech trees and exudes white milk that slowly turns grey-green. It is the most common milkcap in beech forests across Europe and is useful for teaching the concept of host specificity in ectomycorrhizal fungi.

lactarius quietus.story: Named for its 'quiet' or unremarkable appearance, this milkcap has a distinctive oily, bug-like smell that is hard to describe but once learned, never forgotten. It is the most common Lactarius species in European oak forests.

lactarius tabidus.story: This small, common birch-associated milkcap exudes white milk that slowly turns yellow on a white handkerchief — a useful field test. It is one of the most abundant ectomycorrhizal fungi in birch forests across the Northern Hemisphere.

lepista irina.story: Named for its strong floral scent resembling Iris flowers, this large blewit forms fairy rings in grasslands and open woodland edges. The pleasant perfume is unusual among mushrooms and makes it identifiable by smell alone.

lyophyllum connatum.story: This pure white mushroom grows in tightly packed clusters where the stem bases are fused together. Recent research has shown it may contain weak carcinogenic compounds, leading some European authorities to remove it from lists of recommended edible species.

melanoleuca melaleuca.story: This common grassland mushroom with its smooth, dark brown cap and white gills is found in parks, gardens, and meadows worldwide. The genus Melanoleuca contains dozens of very similar-looking species that are extremely difficult to tell apart without microscopy.

mycena arcangeliana.story: This medium-sized Mycena often grows on dead hardwood and has a distinctive mealy or rancid smell. Named after the Italian botanist Giovanni Arcangeli, it is one of the more robust Mycena species and is common in European deciduous forests.

panaeolus acuminatus.story: This small grassland mushroom with a pointed cap is found worldwide on manured pastures and lawns. When fresh, tiny dewdrop-like water droplets cling to its cap surface, giving it a glittering appearance in morning light.

peziza badia.story: This large, dark brown cup fungus is one of the most common Peziza species on bare soil in woodland paths and banks. When disturbed, mature specimens release visible clouds of spores — a puff that can be triggered by breathing on them.

pholiota alnicola.story: This yellow-green Pholiota grows specifically on dead alder and birch wood, often in clusters near streams and wetlands. Its preference for riparian habitats makes it one of the most frequently encountered mushrooms along forest waterways.

pleurotus cornucopiae.story: Unlike the common oyster mushroom which grows in shelf-like clusters, this species produces deeply funnel-shaped caps with gills running far down a distinct central stem. It is commercially cultivated in parts of Europe and Asia and is considered equal in flavor to P. ostreatus.

pluteus petasatus.story: This large, pale Pluteus with a scaly cap center grows on dead hardwood, sawdust piles, and wood chip mulch. It can reach impressive sizes in nutrient-rich substrates like commercial mulch beds and is one of the most commonly reported mushrooms growing in urban landscaping.

polyporus leptocephalus.story: This small, pale polypore with a distinctive black stem base grows on dead hardwood twigs and small branches. It is one of the most common and widespread polypores in temperate forests but is often overlooked due to its small size.

postia stiptica.story: Named for its intensely bitter taste, this white bracket fungus on dead conifers exudes milky white droplets when fresh. The bitter compounds it produces are thought to deter insect larvae that might compete for the wood substrate.

psathyrella corrugis.story: This delicate brown mushroom with a distinctive red-brown gill edge is extremely common on dead wood and soil in gardens and forests. The reddish-brown edge of the gills is visible to the naked eye and is the best field character for identification.

psilocybe stuntzii.story: Named after University of Washington mycologist Daniel Stuntz, this Pacific Northwest species grows on wood chips and lawns on college campuses and parks. It is one of the most commonly encountered psilocybin mushrooms in the Seattle area.

ramariopsis kunzei.story: This small, pure white coral fungus grows in grasslands and is one of the few coral fungi that is a grassland indicator species. Its presence signals ancient, unfertilized pasture — the same type of habitat indicated by waxcap mushrooms.

rhodocollybia butyracea.story: The greasy, buttery feel of this common mushroom's cap is its most distinctive feature. It is one of the most abundant mushrooms in conifer forests across the Northern Hemisphere, often carpeting the forest floor in late autumn.

russula fellea.story: This honey-colored Russula smells distinctly of geraniums or pelargoniums and tastes extremely bitter. The combination of geranium scent and bitter taste makes it one of the easiest Russulas to identify, though it is completely unpalatable.

stemonitis axifera.story: These clusters of chocolate-brown stalks topped with fuzzy spore masses look like miniature bottle brushes growing on dead wood. As the spore case breaks down, it reveals an intricate internal lattice structure that has inspired architectural designers.

suillus cavipes.story: True to its name, this bolete has a distinctly hollow stem and grows exclusively under larches. Its strict host fidelity means it can be found anywhere larches have been planted — from natural forests to urban parks — but never without its tree partner nearby.

tapinella panuoides.story: This fan-shaped brown fungus on dead conifer wood is frequently mistaken for an oyster mushroom but is toxic. It grows on the wood of structures and buildings as well as in forests, and its ability to decay construction timber makes it economically significant.

tricholoma populinum.story: This large, fleshy mushroom grows exclusively under cottonwoods and poplars in western North America. In some regions, particularly around the Great Salt Lake in Utah, it is commercially harvested and sold in farmers' markets as a regional delicacy.

chlorophyllum rachodes.story: Once placed in Macrolepiota alongside the true Parasol Mushroom, DNA studies moved this species to Chlorophyllum. It is edible for most people but causes gastrointestinal upset in a significant minority, making it one of the most polarizing "edible" mushrooms among foragers.

amanita novinupta.story: Named 'new bride' in Latin because its white cap slowly blushes pink, like a bride at the altar. Until 1994, every field guide on the Pacific Coast called it by the wrong name. It shares its California oak woodland habitat with one of the deadliest mushrooms in North America.

gliophorus chromolimoneus.story: This brilliant lemon-yellow waxcap is found almost exclusively in New Zealand and parts of Australia — one of the few mushroom species endemic to Australasia. Its slimy, translucent cap glows like a tiny lantern on the forest floor.

cortinarius vanduzerensis.story: Named after the Van Duzer Forest Corridor in Oregon where it was first collected, this striking mushroom combines a glossy brown cap with a vivid lilac-purple stem. It is found almost exclusively in the old-growth forests of the Pacific Northwest.

gymnopilus viridans.story: One of the rarest Gymnopilus species, known from only a handful of collections in the Pacific Northwest. It gets its name from the greenish stains that develop on its flesh when bruised — a reaction linked to its psilocybin content.
